_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning that the real number system includes both rational and irrational numbers, and we can represent all real numbers on the number line.

_source.w: We are learning that the real number system includes both rational and irrational numbers, and we can represent all real numbers on the number line.
y: Understanding the real number system gives us a complete picture of the numbers available for measurement and calculation — every point on the number line corresponds to a real number.
Distinguishing between rational and irrational numbers deepens our understanding of number structure and prepares us for work with surds and limits.
The real number system is the foundation for calculus, analysis and all advanced mathematics.
eg: Demonstrating that between any two rational numbers, there are infinitely many irrational numbers.
Locating √2 on the number line using a geometric construction (diagonal of a unit square).
Explaining why repeating decimals are rational (they can be written as fractions) while non-repeating, non-terminating decimals are irrational.
Discussing whether the sum of two irrational numbers is always irrational (counterexample: √2 + (–√2) = 0).
Representing the relationship between number sets: natural ⊂ whole ⊂ integer ⊂ rational ⊂ real.
